output:
**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Does replacing TCN's architecture-constant receptive field with a per-position content-gated dilation mechanism (ARF-TCN) recover variable-horizon generalization—where a fixed-dilation TCN provably fails—while retaining the parallelism and gradient-stability advantages over RNNs documented in the original benchmark suite?

---

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

The TCN paper (Bai, Kolter & Koltun, 2018) established a striking empirical result: a generic dilated-causal-convolutional network matches or outperforms LSTMs and GRUs across polyphonic music modeling, character- and word-level language modeling, and carefully chosen synthetic stress tests. Yet this result contains a latent asymmetry it never fully resolves: the RNN's hidden state is, in principle, an unbounded summary of all preceding tokens, while the TCN's receptive field is an architecture-level constant determined at design time, not at inference time. These two inductive biases are not measured against each other on equal footing, because every task in the original benchmark suite has a single, fixed required horizon baked in by construction. A paper that exposes this confound and closes it—by giving convolutional networks a mechanism to adapt their effective memory depth per position and per input—would therefore advance the field on four distinct dimensions.

First, it resolves the core bounded-versus-unbounded-memory asymmetry that the TCN paper exposes but leaves open. The LSTM/GRU hidden state offers theoretically unbounded memory at the cost of sequential computation and vanishing-gradient fragility; the TCN receptive field offers bounded but highly parallel and stably trainable memory. These are treated as a binary trade-off. ARF-TCN dissolves that trade-off by making the effective depth a per-position, content-conditioned quantity—the alpha-weighted mixture of K=4 parallel dilated branches with a shared depthwise backbone. The provable recovery result (uniform alpha = fixed-dilation TCN) confirms ARF-TCN is a strict generalization rather than a different point on the same frontier. A paper establishing this connection would give sequence-modeling researchers a single architecture that does not force an a priori choice between the two regimes.

Second, ARF-TCN reframes "effective memory" as an explicit, measurable, learnable, and interpretable quantity rather than an implicit architectural parameter. The memory-budget regularizer R = λ · E_t[Σ_k α_k(t)·d_k] is not merely a training heuristic; it converts learned dilation weights into an estimable, per-position horizon field whose correlation with ground-truth delay can be evaluated (Spearman ρ > 0.7 as a quantitative bar) and whose failure modes can be diagnosed via placebo control (shuffled alpha must not track horizons). This contribution is reusable beyond ARF-TCN itself: the variable-horizon copy probe and the four-rung evaluation ladder (Oracle, Placebo, Recovery, Leakage) constitute a methodological testbed that any future sequence-model paper—convolutional, recurrent, attention-based, or state-space—can adopt to measure whether its effective memory horizon is actually adaptive or merely wide. The field has been debating convolutions versus recurrence versus attention since at least WaveNet (van den Oord et al., 2016) and the Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017); ARF-TCN offers an interpretable diagnostic lens that reframes that debate around a measurable quantity rather than architecture family.

Third, the practical impact is broad. Efficient variable-context sequence modeling is a persistent bottleneck across audio synthesis, streaming language modeling, music generation, genomics, time-series forecasting, and on-device inference. The O(L²) attention mechanism of Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) is computationally prohibitive for long sequences in resource-constrained settings; linear-recurrent state-space models such as S4 (Gu et al., 2021) and Mamba (Gu & Dao, 2023) trade a different set of engineering constraints. ARF-TCN retains the fully parallel convolutional computation graph—no sequential recurrence, cache-friendly memory access, amenable to modern hardware parallelism—while recovering adaptive-horizon behavior via a lightweight per-position controller (a single linear layer plus softmax). The optional parallel-scan extension (O(L log L)) additionally provides a principled path to unbounded-reach when needed, without breaking the parallelism guarantee for the base model. For practitioners who need stable, efficient training on variable-length or variable-horizon inputs, this represents a concrete and deployable advance.

Fourth, the methodological contribution stands independently of whether ARF-TCN turns out to be the definitive answer to the memory-horizon question. The variable-horizon copy probe—where per-example required delay is drawn from a uniform distribution rather than fixed—is the first controlled experiment that isolates the confound the TCN benchmark suite left implicit. Pair it with the falsifier ladder, and future papers have a rigorous template for claiming adaptive-memory behavior rather than merely demonstrating strong average performance on fixed-horizon benchmarks. This is the kind of diagnostic infrastructure—analogous to calibration benchmarks in uncertainty estimation or compositional generalization tests in NLP—that accelerates an entire subfield by raising the epistemic bar on what "adaptive memory" must demonstrably mean.

---

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The ARF-TCN proposal sits at the intersection of seven distinct technical hardness axes, each of which independently demands a non-trivial solution; together they create a design space with few safe corners.

**1. Differentiable control over an inherently discrete routing decision.**
The ideal mechanism at each position t would select a single dilation index k* = argmax_k f(x_t; θ_c) — a discrete, non-differentiable operation. Backpropagation cannot flow through argmax, so hard selection must be approximated. The soft-mixture approach (softmax α_k(t) with output Y(t) = Σ_k α_k(t)·Branch_k(t)) restores differentiability but requires computing *all* K branches at every position, inflating FLOPs approximately K-fold relative to a single-branch fixed-TCN. Gumbel-softmax / straight-through estimators (the ARF-TCN-Gumbel ablation) alleviate this by stochastically sampling a near-one-hot α̃_k(t) during training, but introduce additional gradient variance and a temperature τ that must be annealed: at large τ the selection is near-uniform (no adaptive gain); at small τ gradients through the reparameterization vanish (training collapses). Correctly scheduling τ across diverse tasks—copy memory, language modeling, music—without task-specific tuning constitutes an unsolved hyperparameter problem; there is no principled closed-form τ schedule known to work across this task diversity.

**2. Preserving full convolutional parallelism while making per-position dilation content-dependent.**
The core TCN advantage over RNNs is that all sequence positions are computed simultaneously via batched, stride-1 convolutions on modern GPU hardware. Naïve per-position dynamic dilation — where each position independently selects its dilation kernel at runtime — breaks this: GPU convolution kernels require a *single fixed dilation* across the batch and sequence for efficient CUDA execution. A position-wise gather-scatter over K dilated feature maps is theoretically correct but requires custom CUDA kernels or inefficient tensor indexing that typically runs 3–10× slower than batched conv, negating the throughput advantage entirely. ARF-TCN sidesteps this by pre-computing K parallel branches with *fixed* dilations {1, 2, 8, 32} (batched conv over K channels) and applying a learned pointwise mix — preserving parallelism by construction. The price is that *all* K branches must be materialized regardless of which branch dominates α_k(t), unless the Gumbel-hard selection ablation is used (which then requires careful FLOP accounting). Even within the parallel-branch design, the mixing-weight network α_k(t) = softmax(W_c·h_t) must itself be computed causally: the local feature h_t from which α_k(t) is derived must not incorporate any future context, adding a separate causal constraint on the controller pathway.

**3. Strict causal masking correctness across K branches with heterogeneous dilations.**
In a fixed-dilation TCN, a single padding scheme ensures causality uniformly. With K branches at dilations d_1 < d_2 < d_3 < d_4, each branch has a different receptive field geometry: branch k covers positions {t, t-d_k, t-2d_k, …, t-(W-1)·d_k} per layer. For large d_k and shallow t, nominal receptive positions reach before the sequence start and must be zero-padded, not zero-masked—a qualitatively different operation. If shared-weight depthwise convolutions are applied across branches via a single grouped-conv operation (a natural optimization), it is easy to misconfigure the padding and accidentally allow the large-dilation branch to see future positions through asymmetric causal padding. A causal-violation bug of this form would not manifest in training loss — the model would silently use future information and appear to outperform all baselines, invalidating every comparison. The only guard is a construction-level unit test: feed a synthetic sequence S where all positions t > t₀ are nonzero and all positions t ≤ t₀ are zero; assert that all K branches produce zero output at position t₀ to within floating-point precision. This test must be run per branch, per dilation, per kernel width, and per number of stacked blocks — it cannot be inferred from architecture inspection alone.

**4. The unbounded-reach extension risks reintroducing the exact RNN pathologies the TCN was designed to avoid.**
The optional parallel-scan linear-recurrent global summary (the +scan ablation) is motivated by the observation that even dilation 32 with L stacked blocks gives a finite receptive field, and some tasks may require unbounded reach. However, *any* recurrent operation reintroduces sequential computational dependency unless implemented via an associative-scan algorithm (Blelloch 1990; Smith et al. 2023), which reduces depth to O(log L) at cost O(L log L). More critically, if the global recurrence is nonlinear — as in standard LSTM/GRU cells — vanishing/exploding gradients over long sequences return immediately. The specific constraint of *linear* recurrence (h_t = A·h_{t-1} + B·x_t with |λ_max(A)| < 1 for stability) is necessary but limiting: the expressiveness of the global summary is capped by linear dynamics. Furthermore, in a jointly-trained multi-component system the coupling between the scan summary and the convolutional branches can destabilize training — the scan can "take over" and the branch mixing collapses to ignoring branches entirely, reproducing a degenerate linear RNN baseline. Disentangling these failure modes requires the full ablation hierarchy (ARF-TCN vs ARF-TCN+scan vs ARF-TCN-Gumbel vs ARF-TCN-fixed) under identical capacity constraints.

**5. Controller collapse: learning WHEN long memory is needed is a hard exploration problem.**
This is the dominant practical risk. On standard fixed-horizon tasks (copy memory with fixed T, regular language modeling), the task-loss gradient through α_k(t) provides essentially no signal that differentiates "use dilation 32 now" from "use dilation 1 now" — the fixed-TCN baseline already works well, so the controller has no incentive to specialize. The typical outcome is α_k(t) ≈ 1/K for all t, which reduces ARF-TCN to a linear combination of K fixed-dilation branches — a strictly wider-coverage fixed-TCN with adaptive gain exactly zero. The memory-budget regularizer R_budget = λ·E_t[Σ_k α_k(t)·d_k] creates pressure toward shorter dilations, but at the cost of a λ that must be swept: too large forces always-short (hurts tasks requiring long memory); too small provides no collapse-prevention signal. Crucially, α-entropy H(α(t)) = −Σ_k α_k log α_k is a necessary diagnostic but not a sufficient one: low entropy (peaked distribution) can indicate healthy specialization *or* degenerate single-branch collapse. Distinguishing these requires cross-referencing entropy with per-timestep correlation between the argmax branch and the task-required dilation — measurable only on synthetic probes (Variable-Horizon Copy) where ground-truth required horizon is known. On natural-language tasks, collapse cannot be directly verified, only inferred from downstream metrics.

**6. Capacity/FLOP matching discipline and the adaptivity-vs-capacity confound.**
K=4 parallel branches with shared depthwise weights reduce parameters relative to K independent full-conv branches, but the FLOPs per position are still ~K× a single-branch fixed-TCN. Without an explicit, documented FLOP-matching protocol, any gain of ARF-TCN over fixed-TCN in the main table is confounded: the model may simply be doing more computation. Standard parameter counting is insufficient because the controller linear layer adds parameters invisible to a naive channel-dim count. The matching protocol must equate: (i) total multiply-adds per sequence position (MACs), accounting for all K branch convolutions plus the controller linear layer; and (ii) total trainable parameters. The fixed-TCN comparator must have its hidden dimension scaled up (wider channels) to meet the MAC budget — which changes its architecture non-trivially and requires its own hyperparameter search. Even with perfect FLOP matching, the K-branch mixture has a structurally richer hypothesis class (it can represent K distinct specialized behaviors) that a single wider fixed-TCN cannot. This structural advantage must be separated from the claimed *per-position adaptive* gain using the Placebo falsifier; without it, any positive result conflates architecture richness with adaptive routing.

**7. Evaluation confounds on the Variable-Horizon Copy probe.**
The new Variable-Horizon Copy task — where required delay d(i) ~ Uniform{T_min, T_max} varies per example — must be constructed to avoid several circular confounds. First, T_max must be set *above* the effective receptive field of fixed-TCN, which is an architecture-determined constant (RF = 1 + 2(W-1)·Σ_{l=0}^{L-1} 2^l for L blocks with kernel width W); this boundary must be pre-computed and explicitly verified before any experiment is run. Second, for the Leakage (OOD) probe, the test delay values must be *disjoint* from all training delay values — requiring a disjointness audit of the train/test delay grid. Third, the ground-truth required-horizon label is a per-example scalar d(i), but α_k(t) is a per-position vector — aggregating α_k(t*) into a scalar predicted horizon â(i) = Σ_k α_k(t*)·d_k must be done at the prediction position t* using a pre-registered aggregation rule. Fourth, without the Placebo falsifier, a model that learned a sequence-length cue (not a content cue) would spuriously appear to track the ground-truth horizon — the Placebo (position-shuffled α across positions within each sequence) must control for exactly this spurious correlation.

**Summary of why naive approaches fail.** Discrete selection is non-differentiable; soft mixture is differentiable but K×-expensive and collapse-prone; weight-sharing reduces parameters but not FLOPs; an unbounded-reach recurrence reintroduces RNN training pathologies unless carefully constrained; and even a correctly implemented ARF-TCN may produce zero adaptive gain on standard benchmarks if the controller collapses to uniform alpha — making the Variable-Horizon probe the essential diagnostic that separates "worked and adapted" from "worked but didn't adapt."

---

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The answer lies at the intersection of four barriers: a design choice that was never questioned, a benchmark that made the limitation untestable, the absence of the right enabling tools until recently, and a mismatch between the approaches that did exist and the specific constraint of causal parallelism.

**The design choice: fixed dilation as an unchallenged constant.** Every major convolutional sequence model that preceded ARF-TCN treats the dilation schedule as a global hyperparameter chosen once at model design time and applied identically at every position and for every input. WaveNet (van den Oord et al., 2016) uses a fixed exponential dilation schedule (1, 2, 4, ..., 512) cycling over residual blocks. ByteNet (Kalchbrenner et al., 2016) uses a similar fixed dilated pattern for machine translation. The gated convolutional network (Dauphin et al., 2017) uses fixed-stride convolutions. ConvS2S (Gehring et al., 2017) likewise. The TCN of Bai et al. (2018) formalizes this as a principled architecture with a provably bounded receptive field—but the bound is a global constant, not a per-position variable. None of these works raised the question of whether the dilation itself could be made content-dependent. This is partly because the standard motivation for dilated convolutions is to increase the *static* receptive field efficiently (polynomial in depth), not to select among multiple possible horizons at runtime.

**The benchmark barrier: the limitation was literally untestable.** This is the most subtle and consequential gap. The copy memory task—arguably the stress test most directly measuring temporal reach—uses a *fixed* delay T (e.g., T = 1000) for every example in every split. The adding problem similarly uses a fixed sequence length. Polyphonic music and language modeling operate on sequences where the relevant context length is approximately constant across examples within a split. Because every classic benchmark task implicitly assumes a single required horizon, a model that matches the right fixed receptive field will perform on par with a model that adaptively selects it: there is no signal differential between the two behaviors. The confound is not an oversight by Bai et al.—it reflects an accurate characterization of the tasks available at the time—but it means the TCN's bounded-memory limitation is *invisible* within the original suite. No prior work designed a variable-horizon probe to isolate this dimension because no prior work was trying to make the horizon adaptive. The new variable-horizon copy task (delay d ~ Uniform per example) is the missing experimental instrument; without it, the problem cannot even be stated as falsifiable.

**The tool gap: discrete selection without breaking differentiability.** Even if a researcher in 2016–2017 had wanted to route to different dilation branches based on input content, they would have faced a hard choice: soft averaging over branches (differentiable but loses the sparsity that makes adaptive computation interpretable) or hard selection (non-differentiable, requires REINFORCE-style estimation with high variance). The Gumbel-softmax trick (Jang et al., 2017; Maddison et al., 2017, ICLR 2017) provided a principled, low-variance reparameterization for discrete categorical choices that is compatible with standard backpropagation. Without it, the Gumbel ablation (ARF-TCN-Gumbel) that recovers FLOP efficiency via hard selection would require either non-differentiable training or continuous relaxations with poor gradient signal. Similarly, the optional parallel-scan extension that provides O(L log L) unbounded-reach via linear recurrence relies on associative-scan formulations that, while known algorithmically since Blelloch (1990), only became practically accessible for sequence modeling with the SSM literature (Gu et al., 2021; Smith et al., 2023). These tools were simply not mature enough to be assembled into an ARF-TCN at the time of the original TCN paper.

**The approach mismatch: prior adaptive methods do not preserve causal parallelism.** The closest prior work to ARF-TCN's goal—giving a neural network adaptive, content-conditioned reach—comes from three directions, each of which misses the specific constraint that defines the problem. *Deformable convolutions* (Dai et al., 2017) learn spatially-offset, input-dependent receptive fields, but they are designed for 2-D vision, are explicitly non-causal (they can attend to future positions in the spatial or temporal grid), and have no notion of memory horizon or causal masking. Adapting them directly to causal sequence modeling is not straightforward and was not attempted. *Self-attention and Transformers* (Vaswani et al., 2017) solve the content-based reach problem—any position can attend to any prior position—but at O(L²) time and memory, sacrificing the convolutional efficiency TCN preserves; and they abandon the local convolutional structure entirely, so the comparison is not a controlled ablation of the dilation mechanism. *Adaptive computation time* (ACT; Graves, 2016) adds per-step adaptive halting to RNNs, which addresses how long to continue processing a given input, but it is defined over sequential recurrence and does not apply to parallel convolutional computation graphs at all. None of these works target the specific combination: causal sequence modeling, convolutional parallelism, and per-position content-gated memory-horizon selection within a fixed K-branch mixture. ARF-TCN is the first to identify this combination as a distinct, well-posed problem and to construct a mechanism that satisfies all three constraints simultaneously—using the alpha-controller architecture, the memory-budget regularizer, and the variable-horizon diagnostic to validate that the adaptation is real rather than nominal.

---

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

### Architecture

**K-branch shared-weight dilated module.** Each ARF-TCN block consists of K=4 parallel dilated causal convolutional branches with dilations D = {d_1, d_2, d_3, d_4} = {1, 2, 8, 32}. All K branches share the same depthwise separable convolutional weights W ∈ ℝ^{C×1×W_k} (kernel width W_k, channel dim C), applied independently at each dilation. Formally, for branch k:

    Branch_k(t) = (W *_{d_k} X)(t) = Σ_{j=0}^{W_k-1} W_j · X(t − j·d_k)

where *_{d_k} denotes causal dilated convolution and left-only zero-padding of size (W_k−1)·d_k ensures X(t') = 0 for t' < 0.

**Per-position controller.** A lightweight linear controller takes the local causal feature h_t = LayerNorm(X_t) ∈ ℝ^C (no future look-ahead) and produces mixing weights on the K-simplex:

    α_k(t) = softmax(W_c · h_t + b_c)_k,    k = 1, …, K

where W_c ∈ ℝ^{K×C}, b_c ∈ ℝ^K. The controller is a single linear layer plus softmax — no recurrence, fully parallel.

**Mixture output:**

    Y(t) = Σ_{k=1}^{K} α_k(t) · Branch_k(t)

**Per-branch causal masking verification [provable-by-construction].** Each branch independently enforces strict causality via left-only zero-padding of size (W_k−1)·d_k. Correctness is verified by a construction-level unit test: for each branch k, set X(t') = 0 for t' ≤ t₀ and X(t') = ε ≠ 0 for t' > t₀; assert Branch_k(t₀) = 0 to within floating-point precision. This test runs per branch, per dilation, per kernel width, per stacked block count — it is part of the mandatory reproducibility checklist and must pass before any reported experiment.

**Residual-block integration mirroring Bai et al.** Each ARF-TCN block wraps the dilated mixture in the original TCN residual structure: weight normalization (WeightNorm) applied to W; spatial dropout (rate p, matched per task to the TCN baseline settings of Bai et al.); a 1×1 convolutional residual connection if input/output channel dimensions differ; ReLU nonlinearities applied to Y(t) before the residual addition. Stack depth and channel width are set to match the fixed-TCN FLOP/param budget under the matching protocol below.

**Gumbel-softmax hard-selection variant (ARF-TCN-Gumbel).** Replace softmax with the Gumbel-softmax reparameterization:

    α̃_k(t) = exp((log α_k(t) + g_k) / τ) / Σ_j exp((log α_j(t) + g_j) / τ),    g_k ~ Gumbel(0,1)

Temperature τ is annealed from τ_0 = 1.0 to τ_min = 0.1 (halved every 20 epochs). At evaluation, use straight-through hard argmax so only one branch is computed per position, reducing inference FLOPs to ≈ single-branch level.

**Optional parallel-scan linear-recurrent global summary (ARF-TCN+scan).** A global context vector c_t ∈ ℝ^{C_s} is injected per block via a diagonal linear recurrence:

    c_t = diag(A) ⊙ c_{t-1} + B · X_t,    with A_i = sigmoid(a_i) ∈ (0,1)

enforcing |λ_max(A)| < 1 (stability by construction). The recurrence is computed via Blelloch parallel prefix scan (O(L log L), fully parallelizable). c_t is concatenated to h_t before the controller linear layer, giving the controller access to an unbounded-horizon summary. The scan contribution is ablated by zeroing c_t — its effect is separable.

### Training Objective

The full training objective is:

    L_total = L_task + λ · R_budget + β · R_entropy

- **L_task**: task-specific loss (cross-entropy for sequence classification/LM; MSE for regression; negative log-likelihood for polyphonic music).
- **R_budget = E_t[Σ_k α_k(t) · d_k]**: penalizes large expected dilation, encouraging horizon parsimony. λ is swept over {0, 1e-4, 1e-3, 1e-2}; the value selected on the validation set is used for main-table reporting.
- **R_entropy** (activated only when per-epoch mean H(α(t)) > log(K) − 0.3 nat, indicating near-uniform collapse): R_entropy = −μ · E_t[H(α(t))] with β = 1e-3; this entropy-floor term encourages non-uniform α and is applied only as a collapse-recovery intervention.

**Optimizer and schedule.** Matching Bai et al. exactly: Adam (lr = 2e-3, β_1=0.9, β_2=0.999), gradient clipping at norm 0.35, batch size and training epochs matched per task to the original TCN paper. A secondary lr sweep {2e-3, 1e-3} is run to confirm results are not lr-sensitive.

### Datasets

**Original TCN benchmark suite (all metrics pre-registered):**

| Dataset | Task | Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Copy Memory (T ∈ {10, 50, 100}) | Synthetic: copy marker after fixed delay T | Accuracy (%) |
| Adding Problem (L ∈ {200, 400, 1000}) | Synthetic: regression on sum of two elements | MSE |
| Sequential MNIST | 784-step sequence classification | Accuracy (%) |
| Permuted MNIST | Shuffled 784-step sequence classification | Accuracy (%) |
| PTB char-LM | Character-level language modeling | BPC |
| text8 char-LM | Character-level language modeling | BPC |
| PTB word-LM | Word-level language modeling | Perplexity |
| WikiText-2 word-LM | Word-level language modeling | Perplexity |
| JSB Chorales | Polyphonic music modeling | NLL (nats) |
| Nottingham | Polyphonic music modeling | NLL (nats) |

**NEW: Variable-Horizon Copy (VHC) probe.** A per-example required delay d(i) is sampled i.i.d. from Uniform{T_min, T_max} at both training and test time. Task: copy a length-K marker sequence observed at positions [1, K] to positions [d(i)+1, d(i)+K], with filler tokens elsewhere. Ground-truth required horizon label for sequence i is d(i).

- T_min = 10, T_max = 120. T_max is chosen to exceed the effective receptive field of fixed-TCN (RF = 1 + 2(W−1)·Σ_{l=0}^{L-1} 2^l), which is computed exactly from architecture parameters and verified prior to any experiment.
- **In-distribution test split**: delays d ~ Uniform{10, 120}, same distribution as training.
- **Leakage (OOD) split**: training delays d ∈ {10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60}; test delays d ∈ {70, 80, 90, 100, 110, 120} — strictly disjoint. A disjointness audit is logged as a required reproducibility check.
- **Sequence-length-cue confound control**: all VHC sequences are padded to a uniform length T_max before input, so total sequence length carries no information about d(i). Additionally, a *position-only controller* ablation — α_k(t) = softmax(W_pos · t), receiving absolute position instead of content — is included and must satisfy horizon correlation ρ(â, d) < 0.3. This validates that input *content*, not absolute position, drives ARF-TCN's horizon-tracking signal.

For ARF-TCN, the predicted expected horizon at prediction position t* is: â(i) = Σ_k α_k(t*) · d_k. For fixed-TCN, â(i) = RF (a constant, independent of input). This aggregation rule is pre-registered.

### Baselines and Ablations

All models are **capacity-matched to ARF-TCN** under the following documented protocol:

**FLOP/param matching protocol (mandatory; documented in paper appendix).** Total multiply-adds per sequence position (MACs) are computed for ARF-TCN by accounting for: all K branch convolutions + the controller linear layer + the optional global summary. The fixed-TCN comparator is widened (larger channel dim C_fixed) until its MACs match ARF-TCN MACs to within ±5%. Total trainable parameter counts are equated to within ±5%. Any residual gap is reported. LSTM and GRU comparators are matched by scaling hidden state dimensionality accordingly.

| Model | Description |
|---|---|
| **Fixed-TCN** (primary baseline) | Bai et al. architecture, FLOP/param-matched by widening C |
| **LSTM** | Capacity-matched LSTM |
| **GRU** | Capacity-matched GRU |
| **ARF-TCN-fixed** (Recovery ablation) | ARF-TCN with α_k(t) ≡ 1/K, uniform and frozen |
| **ARF-TCN-Gumbel** | ARF-TCN with Gumbel-softmax hard selection (FLOP-efficient) |
| **ARF-TCN+scan** | ARF-TCN with parallel-scan linear-recurrent global summary |
| **ARF-TCN-position-only** | Controller fed absolute position t instead of content (α_k(t)=softmax(W_pos·t)); confound control — must yield horizon ρ < 0.3 |

All ablations are capacity-matched under the same protocol.

### Metrics and Diagnostics

**Per-task primary metrics**: as listed in the dataset table above.

**Parallelism and throughput**: wall-clock training throughput (sequences/sec) and inference latency (ms/sequence) on a fixed GPU (A100) at the sequence lengths used by Bai et al., for ARF-TCN vs all baselines. Verifies that convolutional parallelism advantage over LSTM/GRU is retained.

**Horizon tracking (VHC probe only)**: Spearman rank correlation ρ(â(i), d(i)) between predicted expected horizon â(i) = Σ_k α_k(t*) d_k and ground-truth required horizon d(i). Computed on both in-distribution and Leakage test splits. Pre-registered target: ρ > 0.7 for ARF-TCN on in-distribution split.

**Length extrapolation**: train on sequences of length L_train; evaluate on {L_train, 2×L_train, 4×L_train}. Report Δ-metric (performance degradation) for ARF-TCN, fixed-TCN, and ARF-TCN+scan.

**Alpha-entropy collapse diagnostic**: per epoch, compute mean H(α(t)) = −Σ_k α_k(t) log α_k(t) over the validation set. Report H-vs-epoch curve for all ARF-TCN variants. Collapse flag: H → log(K) within first 20% of training triggers activation of the entropy-floor term R_entropy.

**Lambda sweep diagnostic**: report VHC accuracy and Spearman ρ as a function of λ ∈ {0, 1e-4, 1e-3, 1e-2} to characterize the budget-regularizer operating range and identify failure modes at extremes.

### Eval Ladder

**Oracle.** Train and test on sequences with d drawn from the same fixed known value (matched-horizon condition). Provides an upper bound on achievable accuracy when the model is horizon-matched at training time. ARF-TCN ≤ Oracle by construction; the gap quantifies the cost of variable-horizon uncertainty. *Controls for:* whether any model could in principle solve the probe given correct horizon information.

**Placebo.** At test time, randomly permute α_k(t) across positions t within each sequence (position shuffle), preserving the empirical marginal distribution of α values but destroying all positional content structure. *Controls for:* whether K-branch capacity (not per-position content-gated routing) explains performance gains. *Required outcome:* Placebo does NOT reproduce ARF-TCN's horizon tracking — ρ(â_placebo, d(i)) must be statistically indistinguishable from 0 (|ρ| < 0.1, p > 0.05 by permutation test) and VHC accuracy must degrade toward fixed-TCN levels. If Placebo tracks nearly as well as ARF-TCN (|ρ_placebo| > 0.5), the adaptive-gain claim must be retracted.

**Recovery [provable-by-construction].** ARF-TCN-fixed (α_k(t) ≡ 1/K) must reproduce fixed-TCN performance to within ε_recovery = 0.1% accuracy / 0.01 BPC / 0.1 ppl / 0.005 NLL on every original benchmark task. Algebraically: with uniform weights Y(t) = (1/K) Σ_k Branch_k(t) — a fixed linear combination of K dilated convolutions — which is a richer hypothesis class than single-branch fixed-TCN, so performance ≥ fixed-TCN is guaranteed; the test verifies the gap is within ε_recovery (no unexpected gain from architecture alone). Deviation > ε_recovery flags a capacity-matching or implementation error. *Controls for:* architecture implementation correctness.

**Leakage.** Evaluate ARF-TCN, fixed-TCN, and Placebo on the VHC Leakage split where test delays d ∈ {70,80,90,100,110,120} are disjoint from all training delays d ∈ {10,20,30,40,50,60}. *Controls for:* whether ARF-TCN generalizes horizon tracking to unseen delay magnitudes (genuine generalization) or memorizes training delay bins. Fixed-TCN is expected to fail for delays > RF; ARF-TCN is conjectured to generalize partially. Spearman ρ on the Leakage split is a secondary metric; no specific numerical target is pre-registered (this is an exploratory OOD probe, not a pass/fail criterion).

### Expected Outcomes

**Headline [pre-registered; epistemic labels applied]:**

1. **[provable-by-construction]** ARF-TCN structurally contains the fixed-TCN's representational capacity: with uniform α ≡ 1/K the block output is a fixed linear combination of the K dilated branches, a hypothesis class that subsumes the matched single-branch fixed-TCN, so ARF-TCN cannot be strictly less expressive. **[conjectured]** Empirically, the *trained* ARF-TCN-fixed reproduces fixed-TCN performance to within ε_recovery = 0.1% accuracy / 0.01 BPC / 0.1 ppl / 0.005 NLL on all original benchmarks (a deviation beyond ε_recovery flags a capacity-matching or implementation error). This empirical-recovery clause is [conjectured] rather than provable because it depends on optimization dynamics, not algebra alone.

2. **[conjectured]** ARF-TCN ≥ fixed-TCN − ε_inferiority on all original benchmarks (non-inferiority), with pre-registered tolerances ε_inferiority: 0.5 BPC (char-LM), 1 ppl (word-LM), 0.01 NLL (polyphonic music), 0.5% accuracy (synthetic/classification tasks). Failure to meet non-inferiority on any benchmark would indicate the adaptive mechanism harms optimization on stationary-horizon tasks and would constitute a negative result requiring reporting.

3. **[conjectured]** ARF-TCN >> fixed-TCN on VHC in-distribution accuracy, with fixed-TCN near chance when max required delay T_max exceeds its effective RF. Expected gap: >20% absolute accuracy at T_max > RF.

4. **[conjectured]** Predicted expected horizon â(i) = Σ_k α_k(t*) d_k tracks ground-truth d(i) with Spearman ρ > 0.7 on in-distribution VHC test. This is the primary quantitative signal that per-position content-gated routing is functioning as intended.

5. **[provable-by-construction — falsifier]** Placebo does NOT track: ρ(â_placebo, d(i)) is statistically indistinguishable from 0 (|ρ| < 0.1, p > 0.05 by permutation test) on VHC in-distribution. This is a hard pass/fail criterion. If Placebo tracks nearly as well as ARF-TCN (|ρ_placebo| > 0.5), the adaptive-gain claim must be retracted and the paper reframed as a capacity result.

6. **[conjectured]** ARF-TCN retains parallelism advantage over LSTM/GRU: training throughput no worse than 2× below fixed-TCN (K=4 branches with shared weights is expected to be ~2–3× slower than single-branch fixed-TCN in wall-clock, not K=4× due to hardware parallelism). ARF-TCN+scan may show modest additional overhead; expected and reported.

7. **[conjectured]** Memory-budget regularizer at optimal λ improves horizon Spearman ρ on VHC without degrading original-benchmark performance beyond ε_inferiority. The λ sweep will identify the operating range; λ=0 provides the unregularized upper bound and λ=1e-2 the collapse-prevention lower bound.

*Calibration note:* The empirical-recovery clause of Outcome 1 and Outcomes 2–4 and 6–7 are labeled [conjectured] because they depend on optimization dynamics, controller collapse behavior, and the degree to which the VHC distributional shifts align with the K=4 dilation grid — none of which are algebraically provable in advance. Outcome 5 is [provable-by-construction] in the specific sense that position-shuffling α destroys all content signal at each position by design; the only uncertainty is whether residual marginal correlation from un-shuffled α values could produce ρ > 0.1, which is why the pre-registered threshold is |ρ_placebo| < 0.1 rather than exactly 0.
